metadata.summary: Four pillars that determine design system success: clear ownership, usable components, strong documentation, and real adoption metrics.

content.title: Design System Success Factors
content.summary: Four pillars that determine whether a design system succeeds: clear ownership (single DRI per pillar, not a committee), usable components (actually adoptable at v0), strong documentation (discoverable, non-decaying), and real adoption (measured across product, brand, and marketing). Ship lean, stress-test, gather feedback, grow iteratively. Tie adoption metrics to perf/a11y baselines so 100% coverage cannot hide quality regressions.

why.p: Most design system failures trace to one of the four pillars: no owner means nobody fixes drift; unusable components mean teams re-create UI off-system; missing documentation means discoverable only by word-of-mouth; adoption not measured means leadership defunds the work. Brad Frost's Atomic Design research and NNG adoption studies both show that governance and measurability predict long-term survival better than component quality.

list.item: Diagnosing why a design system has low adoption (components built but unused).
Bootstrapping a new system and choosing what to ship in v0 vs v1.
Establishing adoption metrics and instrumentation before a design-ops review.
Drafting governance/ownership models when multiple product teams contribute components.
Component-level decisions (button anatomy, color contrast, motion specs) — those need their own methodologies.
Tooling-only choices (Figma vs Penpot, Storybook vs Histoire) — covered by tools methodologies.
Pure brand/marketing systems with no engineering tie-in — adoption metrics assume product UI.

rule.statement: Assign a single DRI (Directly Responsible Individual) per pillar — not a committee. Committees produce zero accountability.
rule.rationale: Brad Frost and NNG adoption research both show that ownership gaps are the primary cause of design system abandonment; a committee diffuses responsibility until nobody acts.

rule.statement: Separate governance (who decides what enters the system) from maintenance (who fixes bugs); these are distinct accountabilities.
rule.rationale: Conflating governance and maintenance creates decision bottlenecks during incidents and diffuse responsibility during roadmap planning.

rule.statement: Ship v0 with core components only (button, input, select, card, modal, typography, color tokens); do not wait for full coverage.
rule.rationale: Waiting for completeness delays adoption; teams re-create components in the gap, creating the parallel system the design system was meant to replace.

rule.statement: Write a deprecation policy before v0 ships, not after the first regret; set the expectation that components can leave the system.
rule.rationale: Without a deprecation policy, every component is implicitly permanent; the system accumulates debt that blocks refactoring.

rule.statement: Every component must have at least one "do" and one "don't" example; generic copy does not count.
rule.rationale: LLM-generated and template-driven documentation tends toward generic copy; concrete examples are the only form that transfers correct usage to new contributors.

rule.statement: Embed Figma library version and code package version in the docs site so designers and developers can see drift at a glance.
rule.rationale: Version drift between design and code is invisible without explicit version display; version mismatch is a leading cause of spec-vs-implementation divergence.

rule.statement: Measure adoption by splitting greenfield vs. legacy code; reporting aggregate coverage overstates progress.
rule.rationale: A 60% aggregate coverage can mean 100% new code + 0% legacy; the legacy gap is where the most user-facing inconsistencies live.

rule.statement: Tie adoption metrics to a perf/a11y baseline so 100% component coverage cannot hide quality regressions.
rule.rationale: A system component that passes usage tracking but ships with a11y violations or render-blocking CSS is worse than a raw HTML element with neither problem.

example.note: Adoption metric split: greenfield vs. legacy
example.code: Greenfield (new code): 94% design-system components Legacy (pre-DS code): 31% design-system components Overall: 61% (misleading without the split)

list.item: Component coverage — percentage of UI using design system components (greenfield and legacy reported separately).
Adoption rate — percentage of product teams actively consuming the system in PRs over the last 30 days.
Contribution rate — external PRs adding or modifying components from teams outside the core DS team.
Bug/issue count — open issues per component per month (quality indicator; rising count signals adoption without confidence).
Design-dev parity — Figma token version vs. code package version; gap measured in tokens added/removed since last sync.

rule.statement: Run "would I use this?" interviews with 5-7 product developers at v0, v1, and v2. Metric numbers confirm trends; a confused developer is the leading signal of impending abandonment.
rule.rationale: Quantitative metrics lag behavioral change by weeks; developer interviews surface usability problems before they appear in adoption numbers.

rule.statement: Run the audit loop quarterly; persist trend deltas to track the "real adoption" slope, not just the current snapshot.
rule.rationale: A point-in-time score without trend data cannot distinguish a stagnant system from a growing one; the slope determines whether investment is working.

rule.statement: Keep humans in the loop on prioritization — agents count accurately but cannot decide what to deprecate without product context.
rule.rationale: Deprecation decisions require knowing which teams depend on a component and what the migration cost is; agents lack this organizational context by default.

example.code: You are ds-pillar-critic. Inputs: <inventory_json>, <metrics_json>, <doc_audit_json>. Score each pillar (Ownership, Usability, Documentation, Adoption) 0-4 with one evidence quote per score from the provided inputs. Output 3-5 concrete actions ordered by ROI. Format: STRICT JSON {scores:{...}, actions:[{pillar, action, owner_hint, effort:"S"|"M"|"L"}]}.

antipattern.description: Component-usage scans count JSX element names, missing semantic equivalents (MyButton wrapping the system Button).
antipattern.reason: Walk the import graph, not just JSX element names; a wrapper that bypasses tokens looks like adoption but defeats the system.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated component documentation is generic and does not cite real repo examples.
antipattern.reason: Force the agent to reference at least one file path from the actual codebase per doc page; generic copy is worse than no doc because it signals the system is maintained but unhelpful.

antipattern.description: Critic agent hallucinating "missing" components not in the inventory.
antipattern.reason: Provide the full inventory file and require the agent to reference items by ID; name-based references hallucinate items that do not exist.

antipattern.description: Doc auditor auto-fixes documentation without human review.
antipattern.reason: Confidently incorrect examples are worse than missing ones; doc fixes always require a human reader who knows correct usage.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Use a multi-agent audit loop: an inventory agent crawls Figma + Storybook + production HTML to extract component usage, a metrics agent computes the adoption KPIs, a critic agent compares results against the four pillars and emits a prioritized backlog of improvements. Keep humans in the loop on prioritization — agents are good at counting, weak at deciding what to deprecate. Re-run quarterly; persist trend deltas to track the "real adoption" slope.

list.item: ds-inventory — sonnet; reconciles components defined (Figma library, code package) vs used (production DOM, screenshots).
ds-metrics-collector — haiku; aggregates raw counts into the five adoption metrics.
ds-doc-auditor — sonnet; reads docs site, flags missing examples, dead links, components without "do/don't" guidance.
ds-pillar-critic — sonnet; scores each pillar 0-4 with evidence and produces an improvement plan.
chromatic CLI — Storybook visual review + adoption telemetry: npm i -D chromatic
storybook CLI — Component catalog, docgen, MDX docs: npx storybook init
omlet-cli (or react-scanner) — Component usage scanner across codebase: npm i -g @omlet/cli
style-dictionary CLI — Token build/distribution for design-dev parity metric: npm i -g style-dictionary
figma-tokens-cli / Tokens Studio CLI — Sync Figma variables ↔ code tokens: github.com/tokens-studio/figma-plugin
lighthouse-ci — Track perf/a11y per component page (quality indicator): github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse-ci
Chromatic (SaaS) — Component coverage, visual diff, usage analytics. Yes to agents (REST + CLI).
Knapsack (SaaS) — Multi-brand DS + governance workflows. Yes to agents (REST).
Supernova (SaaS) — Token sync, doc generation, design-dev parity. Yes to agents (REST).
Zeroheight (SaaS) — Doc hosting; agent-driven doc updates possible. Limited agents (content APIs).
Backstage (Spotify) (OSS) — Treat the design system as a Backstage entity for ownership/SLOs. Yes to agents (plugins).
Omlet (SaaS) — React component analytics across repos. Yes to agents (REST).
Storybook (OSS) — Self-hosted catalog, often the source of truth for "components defined". Yes to agents (Node/JS APIs).
Ship v0 with a deprecation policy already written, not after the first regret. Set the expectation that components can leave the system.
Tie adoption metrics to a perf/a11y baseline so "100% coverage" cannot hide quality regressions.
Make contribution explicit: every PR adding to product UI gets a checkbox "did you check the system?" in the PR template; agents enforce via CI.
Pair each pillar with a single owner role (DRI), not a committee. Committees produce zero accountability.
Run a "would I use this?" interview with 5-7 product devs at v0, v1, v2. Numbers lie; a confused dev is signal.
Embed Figma library version + code package version in the docs site so designers and devs can see drift at a glance.

see-also.link: Nathan Curtis — Measuring Design System Success
Nielsen Norman Group — Design System Adoption articles

templates.template: Shell script that counts design-system component usage vs. raw HTML across TSX files.
Structured-output prompt for a ds-pillar-critic subagent scoring all four pillars.
